retrieved_docs.title: Strongyloidiasis -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Review the life cycle and mechanism of infection of Strongyloides stercoralis. Describe the clinical presentation of a patient infected with Strongyloides stercoralis. Summarize the medical management of a patient infected with Strongyloides stercoralis. Outline the importance of the interprofessional team in advancing care for patients infected by Strongyloides stercoralis. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Ascariasis -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: The life span of an adult worm is two years. They do not multiply in the host. If there is no reinfection by the ingestion of fecal-contaminated food, the patient can clear the infection without treatment. This is why the first line of management should be an education in good hygiene and public health. Hand hygiene is of unmeasurable importance to prevent infection with Ascaris lumbricoides. In areas of high endemicity, early diagnosis and treatment are important to prevent acute complications, and in the long term, prevent malnutrition, cognitive disabilities, and loss of productive years.

retrieved_docs.title: Helminthiasis -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: S. stercoralis infection is commonly asymptomatic, although it may present with eosinophilic pneumonia just like hookworms and A. lumbricoides . Chronic strongyloidiasis might present with anorexia, nausea, weakness, abdominal pain, tenderness, and diarrhea. Larva currens is present as serpiginous urticaria due to chronic infection, mostly over the abdomen, torso, groin, and buttocks. Rarely, reactive arthritis may occur by the immune-mediated mechanisms. Strongyloides hyper-infection syndrome occurs by autoinfection in immunosuppressed individuals who present with intestinal failure or pulmonary failure. If untreated, the mortality ratio is approximately 100%. When large numbers of parasites spread into the different organs of the body, it leads to disseminated strongyloidiasis that present with catastrophic clinical manifestations such as meningitis, disseminated intravascular coagulation, shock, and renal failure. Those who are taking immunosuppressive drugs such as corticosteroid and vincristine, hematological malignancies, hypogammaglobinaemia, and human T-cell lymphotropic virus type 1 infection are vulnerable to develop disseminated strongyloidiasis. [3] [24] [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Hookworm -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Interestingly, in individuals with hookworm infections, same as other helminths, patients tend to have a wider variety of gut microbiota. This observation, along with immune regulatory mechanisms in hookworms, prompted research into the potential use of hookworms to treat immune-mediated gastrointestinal diseases like celiac disease and inflammatory bowel disease. [1] [5] [7] [9] [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Helminthiasis -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: For echinococcosis , most of the patients remain asymptomatic unless complications occur and are diagnosed accidentally. During patient admission, the most common presentation is pain at the right hypochondrium. Mechanisms by which a cyst becomes symptomatic include rupture with resultant infections or anaphylaxis, development of fistula with surrounding structures such as biliary tract, intestine, and bronchus. [32] [33]

retrieved_docs.title: Helminthiasis -- Pathophysiology -- Indirect damage:
retrieved_docs.content: S stercoralis can cause Loeffler syndrome by type 1 hypersensitivity reaction.

retrieved_docs.title: Ascariasis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Ascaris lumbricoides, a soil-transmitted large nematode, causes  Ascariasis. The female adult can reach up to 20 cm to 30 cm in length, and adult males up to 15 cm to 20 cm. The nematodes are pink/yellow/white roundworms. The female worms are thicker and have a straight rear end. The male worm is more slender with a ventrally incurvated rear end with two retractile copulating spicules. The average life of the adult parasite is one year, after which it dies, and it is spontaneously eliminated as it evacuates through the digestive tract. This is the reason why a spontaneous cure of disease can happen if there is no reinfection. Adult worms do not multiply in the human host. [4] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Helminthiasis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Other phyla platyhelminths also called flatworms, which include flukes schistosomes and tapeworms such as the pork tapeworm that causes cysticercosis. Flukes are known as trematodes, and tapeworms are known as cestodes. [1] [6] Soil-transmitted helminthiasis is a roundworm ( Ascaris lumbricoides ), whipworm ( Trichuris trichiura ), and hookworm ( Ancylostoma duodenale and Necator americanus ). [4] [3] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Tapeworm -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Ingestion of cysticerci only causes taeniosis or the intestinal adult tapeworm infection; ingestion of cysticerci does not cause cysticercosis, which is due to the direct ingestion of T. solium eggs passed in the stool due to poor hygiene practices. [3] Autoinfection is possible through this route when an infected person with taeniasis ingests passed Taenia eggs. Embryos hatch in the small intestine then may invade and spread hematogenously to the central nervous system (neurocysticercosis) or muscle and other tissue. Person-to-person spread is more common than previously thought rather than contamination through environmental sources. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Albendazole -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Albendazole sulfoxide, the active metabolite of albendazole, causes selective degeneration of cytoplasmic microtubules in intestinal and tegmental cells of intestinal helminths and larvae. The metabolite binds to the B-tubulin subunit of the helminth's microtubules and thus inhibits microtubule polymerization. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Strongyloidiasis (Archived) -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Parasite pathways are different from the human host and allow selective interference by chemotherapeutic agents. The effectiveness of anthelmintic agents against larvae is poor; they are more effective in established infection.

retrieved_docs.title: Strongyloidiasis -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: As outlined above, the most dreaded consequence of Strongyloides stercoralis infection is hyperinfection syndrome, which is associated with severe disseminated disease and frequently leads to multiorgan failure with eventual death despite anthelmintic therapy. [33] Additionally, patients with severe diseases are also at risk of gram-negative bacteremia and sepsis, which is why broad-spectrum antibiotics are recommended in addition to antihelminthic therapy in patients with severe disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Trichinosis -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Trichinosis presents with eosinophilia and intestinal symptoms in early infection. Therefore, several parasitic infections may cause the patients’ symptoms. These include: strongyloidiasis schistosomiasis hookworm gnathostomiasis lymphatic filariasis ascariasis whipworm

retrieved_docs.content: Taenia solium , the pork tapeworm, frequently develops in the intestine leads to taeniasis, and in the central nervous system leads to cysticercosis. [16] [18] [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Helminthiasis -- Pathophysiology -- Indirect damage:
retrieved_docs.content: Strongyloides and Trichinella may induce prolonged inflammation of the intestine that causes villous atrophy; in severe cases, it may cause protein-losing enteropathy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 758
retrieved_docs.content: Nitroimidazole derivatives, antiprotozoal agents against amoebiasis and other protozoal diseases, constitute a class of nitro‑containing compounds that are employed to treat infections caused by amoebae and various other protozoa. Members of this pharmacologic group include ornidazole, azanidazole, nimorazole, propenidazole, secnidazole and tinidazole, each of which shares the characteristic nitroimidazole core that confers activity against protozoal organisms. By belonging to the same chemical class, these agents exhibit similar mechanisms of action and therapeutic indications, making them useful options in the management of amoebiasis and related protozoal diseases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 88
retrieved_docs.content: the quinoline gynecological anti‑infective class, linking quinoline‑based gynecological therapy with antiprotozoal activity. Other quinoline gynecological anti‑infectives include dijodhydroxychin, an antiprotozoal compound. Triazole‑derived gynecological anti‑infectives include terconazole, which prevents fungal infections. All agents identified as antiprotozoal—acetarsol, albendazole, niridazol, triklabendazol and dijodhydroxychin—are also classified under the “agents against amoebiasis and other protozoal diseases.” Niridazol and triklabendazol are further listed as other antitrematodal agents for trematode infections, while albendazole is used for a wide range of helminthic infections. Antimalarial drugs are a child subclass of antiprotozoal agents, reflecting their shared mechanism against protozoan pathogens. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1519
retrieved_docs.content: Quinoline derivatives and related substances, antitrematodals are classified as members of both the oxamnichin group and the praziquantel group, indicating that these quinoline‑based antitrematodal agents share characteristics with the oxamnichin class and with the praziquantel class of antiparasitic drugs. This dual classification reflects their role as antitrematodal compounds and suggests overlapping mechanisms or therapeutic uses in the treatment of trematode infections.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1681
retrieved_docs.content: Antihelmintika constitute a pharmacological class of substances that are specifically employed to treat infections caused by parasitic worms (paraziti). Within the broader grouping of “substances categorized by disposition,” antihelmintika are recognized as agents directed against helminths. The class includes several individual drugs: levamisol is identified as a child (subtype) of antihelmintika; albendazol, arprinocid, avermectin, clorsulon, febantel, and paromomycin are each classified as types of antihelmintika. By sharing the common mechanism of targeting and eliminating parasitic worms, these agents are integral to the management of helminthic diseases and underscore the importance of anti‑parasitic therapy in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 4 -- Community 2861
retrieved_docs.content: Imidazole derivatives that are used as gynecological antiinfectives constitute a pharmacologic class that includes several specific agents. Ekonazol, flutrimazole, and oxiconazole are all members of the imidazole‑derived gynecological antiinfective group, sharing the common chemical scaffold and therapeutic purpose of treating gynecologic infections. Recognizing that these three compounds belong to the same class helps clinicians understand their similar mechanisms of action, spectrum of activity, and potential cross‑reactivity, thereby informing appropriate selection and management of antiinfective therapy in gynecologic practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 4 -- Community 2865
retrieved_docs.content: Ekonazol, flutrimazole and butoconazole are all classified as imidazole‑derivative gynecological anti‑infectives. Each of these agents belongs to the broader group of imidazole derivatives that are used specifically to treat fungal infections of the female genital tract. By sharing the imidazole chemical scaffold, these drugs provide targeted antimicrobial activity for gynecological indications, and their classification under the imidazole‑derivative gynecological anti‑infective category highlights their common mechanism of action and therapeutic use.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 2434

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1391
retrieved_docs.content: Quinoline derivatives and related substances, antitrematodals are classified as members of both the oxamnichin and praziquantel drug families, indicating that these quinoline‑based antitrematodal agents share characteristics with each of these established antiparasitic compounds. The mechanism by which oxamnichin exerts its therapeutic effect is described only as an Unknown Cellular or Molecular Interaction [MoA], reflecting that the precise cellular or molecular target of oxamnichin has not yet been identified. This relationship underscores that, while quinoline antitrematodals are linked to oxamnichin and praziquantel, the exact mode of action for oxamnichin remains undefined, highlighting an area for further research to improve understanding of its pharmacology and optimize patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 4 -- Community 2892
retrieved_docs.content: Flutrimazole, isoconazole, and tioconazole are all imidazole‑derived compounds that belong to the therapeutic class of gynecological anti‑infectives. As members of the imidazole derivatives, gynecological anti‑infectives group, these agents share a common chemical scaffold and are used to treat fungal infections of the female genital tract. Their classification within the same drug class highlights their similar mechanism of action and clinical role in managing gynecological mycotic infections.

The subclass “Imidazole derivatives, antibacterial for systemic use” comprises ornidazol and tinidazol, which also fall under the “Nitroimidazole derivatives, antiprotozoal agents against amoebiasis and other protozoal diseases” subclass together with propenidazole, nimorazol, secnidazole, and thiofeny, illustrating the overlap between systemic antibacterial and antiprotozoal activities. Ornidazol is further listed as a child concept of antiparasitic agents, emphasizing its antiparasitic activity. Furazolidone is identified as an inverse member of “antiinfekční látky lokální,” denoting its use as a locally applied antimicrobial. Collectively, these relationships delineate a complex taxonomy of antimicrobial, antifungal, antiparasitic, and supportive pharmacologic agents, highlighting shared mechanisms such as decreased protein synthesis, overlapping chemical families (imidazole, triazole, nitroimidazole), and dual

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 4 -- Community 2742
retrieved_docs.content: Imidazole derivatives, gynecological antiinfectives constitute a pharmacologic class of antifungal agents that are employed to treat fungal infections of the female reproductive tract. The drugs fenticonazole, flutrimazole and butoconazole are all members of this class, sharing the imidazole chemical scaffold that interferes with fungal cell membrane synthesis by inhibiting ergosterol production. Because they belong to the same therapeutic group, these agents are used interchangeably or sequentially in the management of conditions such as vaginal candidiasis and other gynecologic mycoses, offering clinicians multiple options within a unified mechanism of action.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 4 -- Community 2751
retrieved_docs.content: Quinoline derivatives and related substances that function as antitrematodals are classified as members of the praziquantel group, indicating that these quinoline‑based compounds share the pharmacologic characteristics of praziquantel and are used in the treatment of trematode infections.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1603
retrieved_docs.content: Nitroimidazole derivatives, described as antiprotozoal agents against amoebiasis and other protozoal diseases, constitute a pharmacologic class that includes several specific compounds. Azanidazole, nimorazol, propenidazole and secnidazole are all members of the nitroimidazole derivative class, sharing the characteristic nitroimidazole chemical scaffold and being employed in the treatment of amoebiasis and various other protozoal infections.
